facts.label: GREEN TO DODGE SCOTLAND
facts.story: The team didn't always wear green. For its first decades the Irish side played in royal blue, but in 1931 the Irish Football Association switched to green shirts, officially to avoid clashing with Scotland's blue. The change stuck permanently, and green has carried the team through every World Cup since, even as the side itself was still simply called 'Ireland' well into the 1970s, decades after partition split the island's football.

facts.label: STILL THE SMALLEST
facts.story: In 1958, Northern Ireland became the least populous country ever to reach a World Cup, a record broken only in raw qualification terms by Trinidad and Tobago in 2006. But Northern Ireland still holds the deeper version of that record: smallest nation to reach a World Cup more than once, to win a match, to score a goal, and to escape the first round — all set by a country of under 1.5 million people.

1958.headline: Smallest Nation, Biggest Run

annotations.label: CAPTAIN, MINUS HIS BROTHER
annotations.story: Danny Blanchflower led Northern Ireland to Sweden four months after the Munich air disaster ended the international career of his younger brother Jackie, a teammate on the same national side. Danny captained the team through a play-off win over Czechoslovakia and into the quarter-finals, the furthest a team of Northern Ireland's size had ever gone.

annotations.label: A SHIRT WITH NO LOGOS
annotations.story: Northern Ireland's green jersey in Sweden carried no sponsor and no manufacturer crest — just heavyweight cotton and a lace-up V-neck collar, the standard cut of the era. Shirt sponsorship in international football was still two decades away; the only branding anyone saw was the Irish Football Association crest above the heart.

annotations.label: FOG, A RIOT, A REPLAY
annotations.story: Northern Ireland's qualifying decider against Italy was first wrecked by fog that stranded the referee and a veto over his English replacement, so the fixture was downgraded to a friendly that ended in a pitch invasion nicknamed the 'Battle of Belfast.' The real qualifier came six weeks later — Northern Ireland won 2–1.

1958.homeColors: green shirt with white V-neck collar, white shorts, blue socks

1982.headline: Shock Win Over the Hosts

annotations.label: ARCONADA'S FUMBLE, ARMSTRONG'S GOAL
annotations.story: Needing a win over host nation Spain at the Mestalla, Northern Ireland got it when goalkeeper Luis Arconada spilled a routine shot straight to Gerry Armstrong, who tucked it in. Down to ten men after Mal Donaghy's 60th-minute red card, Northern Ireland held on to win 1–0 and top their group ahead of the host nation.

annotations.label: THE SHIRT SAID IT ALL
annotations.story: Adidas stitched 'Spain World Cup Finals 1982' directly beneath the Irish FA crest on the home shirt, a rare instance of a tournament kit literally spelling out its own occasion. The away change strip went further stylistically, a white shirt striped with thin green pinstripes that never returned in later Adidas designs.

annotations.label: SEVENTEEN AND UNTESTED
annotations.story: Billy Bingham handed a World Cup debut to 17-year-old Norman Whiteside, who'd played only two competitive games for Manchester United, breaking Pelé's record as the tournament's youngest-ever player. He picked up a yellow card that day too — another record, for youngest player booked at a World Cup.

1982.homeColors: green shirt with white and gold Adidas trim, white shorts
1982.awayColors: white shirt with green Adidas trim

1986.headline: Jennings' Final Bow

annotations.label: OLDEST MAN ON THE PITCH
annotations.story: Goalkeeper Pat Jennings played his final match for Northern Ireland against Brazil in Guadalajara on his 41st birthday, making him the oldest player to appear in a World Cup finals match at the time. Brazil won 3–0, closing out both the tournament and the international career of Northern Ireland's most-capped player.

annotations.label: A CRUCIFORM CREST, NO ADS
annotations.story: Adidas re-lettered the Irish FA's Celtic-cross crest to spell out 'Northern Ireland' across its horizontal bar for Mexico '86, striping the jersey in alternating light and dark green. FIFA's rules barred sponsor logos on tournament shirts entirely, so the trefoil and crest were the only marks allowed on the fabric.

annotations.label: TRAINING FOR MEXICAN HEAT
annotations.story: Weeks before Mexico, Bingham's squad ran hill sprints in Albuquerque, New Mexico, in temperatures above 38°C to prepare for Guadalajara's altitude and heat. The IFA shipped out heat-adapted kit and reportedly stockpiled 10,000 bottles of water for a squad built around a 41-year-old goalkeeper and an 18-year-old attacker.

1986.homeColors: green shirt with white trim (Adidas), white shorts
1986.awayColors: white shirt with green Adidas trim
